insights.title: ASR Team – Phase 3 Time Close Skip Analysis
insights.summary: Analyzed 47 ASR calls from the week of June 16–20. Found near-universal skip of the Phase 3 two-slot time close, with reps jumping from discovery directly to pitch close. This pattern correlated with significantly lower booking conversion.
main_findings: 96% of reps skipped the Phase 3 two-slot time close entirely
Reps who completed Phase 3 converted at 34% vs 18% for those who skipped
Average call time was 2.3 minutes shorter on skipped calls — reps are rushing
Most common skip point: after prospect confirms interest, rep immediately pitches the calendar link
positive_patterns: Discovery questions (Phase 1) executed well across 82% of calls
Tone was warm and conversational — no aggressive closers identified
Product knowledge was solid; reps answered product questions confidently
customer_objections: I need to check my schedule first
Can you just send me the info?
I'm not sure I have time right now
What exactly are we meeting about?
winning_language: 'I want to make sure this is worth your time — I have two slots that work for most folks...'
'Before I pull up the calendar, which works better for you — mornings or afternoons?'
'Most of our members said the same thing before they saw the full picture — 20 minutes is all it takes.'
pain_points: Golfers feel they've already tried 'everything' to improve
Time commitment concerns around training programs
Skepticism about online/digital coaching vs in-person
buying_triggers: Mention of specific handicap improvement numbers
Peer social proof ('other golfers just like you')
Money-back guarantee mention
Limited slot / urgency framing
opportunities: Implement mandatory Phase 3 checkpoint in QA rubric
Create a two-slot time close script card for ASR reps
Add Phase 3 as a scored item in AI QA rubric immediately
recommendations: Update QA rubric to score Phase 3 as a required checkpoint (not optional)
Run targeted Phase 3 role-play session in next team training
Add time-close language to the Master Script Hub sidebar
Flag any call under 9 minutes for supervisor review

action_items.task: Update AI QA rubric to include Phase 3 scoring
Create Phase 3 two-slot time close script card
Schedule team role-play session

transcript_excerpts.label: Example of skipped Phase 3 (common pattern)
transcript_excerpts.text: Rep: 'Great, so you're interested in improving your short game?' Prospect: 'Yeah, definitely.' Rep: 'Perfect, let me go ahead and send you a calendar link for the call!' [Phase 3 skipped entirely]

transcript_excerpts.label: Example of Phase 3 done correctly
transcript_excerpts.text: Rep: 'Perfect. Before I pull this up — do mornings or afternoons tend to work better for you?' Prospect: 'Afternoons are easier.' Rep: 'Great. I've got Tuesday at 2pm or Thursday at 3pm — which one works?' Prospect: 'Thursday works.' [Booked.]

insights.title: Retention Team – Billing Surprise as #1 Cancellation Driver
insights.summary: Retention call analysis across June 8–18 reveals that billing surprise around the $29/month Scratch Club subscription is the top stated reason for cancellation. Approximately 48% of callers expressed some form of 'I didn't know I was going to be charged.' Save rate currently running at 43% vs the 60% target.
main_findings: 48% of cancellation calls cited billing surprise as primary reason
Save rate at 43% MTD vs 60% target — 17-point gap
Refund rate well above 12% goal
65% of retention calls had zero golf context established before pitching the save offer
Reps are leading with price defense rather than value re-anchoring
positive_patterns: Reps who led with empathy and golf context first had a 61% save rate
Scratch Club value proposition resonated when explained clearly with specifics
Tone was generally calm and non-confrontational
customer_objections: I didn't know I was being charged $29 a month
I thought this was a one-time thing
I haven't even used it
It's not worth it for me right now
I'm just not playing golf right now
winning_language: 'I completely understand — let me take a look at your account and see what makes the most sense for you.'
'A lot of members feel that way before they actually log in — can I show you what's actually in there?'
'What's your game like right now? Are you still getting out much?'
pain_points: Customers feel deceived by unclear billing communication at point of sale
Low perceived usage / value in first 30 days
No personal connection to the product content
buying_triggers: Specific feature they haven't tried yet
Pause option instead of cancel
Partial refund as goodwill gesture
Connecting product to their specific golf goal
opportunities: Implement a 'golf context first' protocol before any save pitch
Create a billing expectation reset script for reps
Explore pause/hold option to reduce hard cancellations
Work with fulfillment to improve Day 1 onboarding email clarity
recommendations: Add mandatory golf context question before any retention pitch
Script a billing reset empathy sequence for reps (not defensive, empathetic)
Propose a 'pause for 30 days' offer as a save tool
Flag billing surprise calls for upstream marketing/checkout team

action_items.task: Draft billing surprise empathy script for retention team
Propose pause feature to Jeff/Denver
Audit Day 1 onboarding email for billing clarity

transcript_excerpts.label: Most common billing surprise opening
transcript_excerpts.text: Customer: 'I want to cancel. I didn't know I was going to be charged $29.' Rep: 'I understand. The charge is for your Scratch Club membership which you signed up for...' [Defensive framing — customer disengages]

insights.title: Closer Team – Top 3 Objection Types and Call Position Timing
insights.summary: Closer call review for June identified the top three objection categories by frequency and call-position timing: cost/spend fatigue (appearing at 28+ minute mark), equipment resistance (mid-call, 15–20 min), and video/technology skepticism (early call, 8–12 min). Understanding timing allows reps to pre-empt rather than react.
main_findings: Cost/spend fatigue is #1 objection — 67% of non-converted calls included it
Equipment resistance peaks at 15–20 minute mark — typically after program reveal
Video/tech skepticism surfaces early (8–12 min) and if unhandled, kills the call
Reps who pre-empted cost objection before it arose converted at 2.1x rate
Only 22% of reps used any pre-emption language in June
positive_patterns: Reps with strong rapport-building in first 10 minutes had 40% higher close rates
Story-based selling (member transformation stories) outperformed feature-listing
Reps who acknowledged skepticism and leaned in rather than defending closed better
customer_objections: I've already spent so much on golf stuff that didn't work
I'm not sure I need new equipment on top of this
I'm not great with video calls / technology
Can I think about it?
My wife/partner needs to know about this first
winning_language: 'Before we get into the investment — a lot of our members told me the same thing. Let me show you why this is different.'
'The technology piece is honestly the easiest part — our team walks you through everything in the first session.'
'You don't need any new equipment. This works with exactly what you have in your bag right now.'
pain_points: Past purchase regret — golfers have wasted money on gear, lessons, apps before
Tech anxiety — older demographic uncomfortable with video coaching
Spouse approval required — purchase decisions not made independently
buying_triggers: Specific transformation story matching their handicap range
No-equipment-needed confirmation
Guarantee / risk reversal language
Coach expertise and credibility mention
Limited enrollment / cohort framing
opportunities: Build objection pre-emption scripts for each of the top 3 objections
Create a 'tech onboarding assurance' talking point for video-skeptical prospects
Develop spouse/partner inclusion script for end-of-call scenarios
Train reps to identify which objection type is dominant in first 10 minutes
recommendations: Add objection pre-emption module to closer onboarding and monthly training
Create a 'Top 3 Objections Cheat Sheet' with timing guidance for each
Score pre-emption language in QA rubric for closer calls
Analyze non-converted calls specifically for unhandled tech skepticism

action_items.task: Build Top 3 Objections pre-emption script card
Add pre-emption scoring to closer QA rubric
Present findings to closer team in weekly meeting

transcript_excerpts.label: Cost objection handled with pre-emption (winning example)
transcript_excerpts.text: Rep: 'Before I walk you through what this looks like investment-wise — I want to acknowledge something. A lot of the guys I talk to have spent money on things that didn't deliver. So I want to be upfront about why this is structured differently.' [Prospect engagement increases — less defensive when cost revealed]

insights.title: Internal Setter Team – June 24 Full-Day Transcript Analysis (907 Calls)
insights.summary: Full analysis of 907 outbound Internal Setter calls placed on June 24, 2026. Of 907 total calls, 796 (87.8%) were unreachable or voicemail. Of the 111 live contacts, 27 resulted in a booked appointment (24.3% live-contact conversion), 30 declined, and 10 had follow-up potential. The top 3 decline drivers were cost, time/schedule conflict, and location limitations. Price objections surfaced as early as the opening minute on several calls. Reps who completed full discovery before revealing price averaged 10.9-minute calls and converted; those who answered cost questions immediately averaged 4.1-minute calls and lost the lead. Five Scratch Club cancellation calls were handled with zero save attempts.
main_findings: 907 total calls; 796 (87.8%) unreachable or VM — only 111 live contacts made
27 booked out of 111 live contacts = 24.3% live-contact conversion rate
Booked calls averaged 10.9 minutes vs 4.1 minutes for declined — a 2.6x talk-time gap that directly tracks with conversion
Top 3 decline types: Declined-Cost (6 calls), Declined-Time (6 calls), Declined-Location (4 calls)
Price objections appeared in the first 60 seconds on 3 of 4 cost-decline calls — before any discovery was run
Location objections were hard-stop exits — zero reps successfully converted a location objection in this dataset
Julius Vizcayno led all reps in declines (7) with only 2 bookings; Heinrich Abarquez had 5 declines and 1 booking — both need objection coaching
Dan Emmanuel Nicolas led the team in bookings (7) with only 3 declines — the highest conversion efficiency on the team
5 Scratch Club cancellation calls processed — zero retention attempts made on any of them
Canadian and international leads received the same pitch as domestic leads despite location and currency barriers making them unlikely to convert
positive_patterns: Reps who deflected the price question and ran full discovery before revealing cost had significantly longer calls and higher conversions
Phoebe Collado's booked call (Jane Dunwoodie, TMA Booked) demonstrated the full script structure correctly: warm open, full discovery, root-flaw framing, time close to Saturday Zoom
Dan Emmanuel Nicolas showed consistent booking efficiency — the highest booked-to-declined ratio on the team (7 bookings / 3 declines)
Social proof framing with 9,000 golfers and top-10-coach credibility was deployed well on booked calls
Root-flaw language ('we find the root cause underneath it all') was the strongest differentiator when used — it visibly shifted prospect engagement
customer_objections: "I was really just fishing to see what the price point is." — Richard Ian Alvarez call, stated in opening
"I'd rather just cut to the chase — if it's a price point I can't afford, there's no sense going through my game." — Glenn Huth
"Wow, okay, yeah." — after hearing $3,000–$4,000, call declined within seconds
"I'm not going to Florida or Texas or California to take a golf lesson. I want something right here in St. Louis, Missouri." — Cathy York
"I can't take 5 days and go to Florida to a golf academy. I'm not in a position to do that because of demands in my profession." — C. Randolph Keller
"I didn't even know that I was a member. I'd like to cancel my membership. You guys keep taking money out of my account automatically." — Ho yean Song (Scratch Club)
"I'm getting a bill every month, so I would like to cancel the one I'm getting charged for." — Louise Finch (Scratch Club)
winning_language: "We've worked with over 9,000 golfers. One thing that separates us from local lessons or YouTube is we don't just fix the miss — we find the root cause, the one thing underneath it all that keeps pulling everything else out." (Phoebe Collado — TMA booked)

"There's really two kinds of golfers: guys who enjoy the game, and guys who, if they're honest, know they're capable of playing better and are actually ready to do something about it. Which one are you?" (Phoebe Collado — strong identity trigger)

"Since everything is customized, same goes with the cost — cost varies from person to person. The reason I called is to make sure the program is a good fit for you first." (price deflection that enables discovery)
"What's your goal from a score standpoint within a year?" (score-goal anchor — used well on all booked calls)
pain_points: Chipping and short game — most cited pain point across both booked and declined calls
Driver inconsistency — second most common: slice, push-fade, direction control
Time poverty — many leads have genuine interest but work schedules, family obligations, and caregiving are real blockers
Location friction — U.S.-only academy footprint is a hard ceiling for Canadian prospects and golfers in underserved states
Price shock — the $3,000–$4,000 price point landing cold, without value framing or discovery, triggers immediate exit
Scratch Club billing confusion — recurring subscription charges not remembered or understood by multiple customers
buying_triggers: Root cause framing — 'we find the root flaw, not just the miss' was the strongest engagement trigger on booked calls
Customization language — 'everything is built around your specific game' reduced price resistance before the number was revealed
Coach credibility — 'top 10, top 20 coaches in the US' created perceived value that justified the investment
Score goal anchoring — getting the prospect to name their 1-year goal made them emotionally invested before the pitch
Social proof at scale — '9,000 golfers' created safety and credibility
opportunities: PRICE PRE-EMPTION SCRIPT: Build a confident redirect for when prospects ask about cost before discovery is complete
LOCATION QUALIFIER AT OPEN: Add a state/location screen to the first 2 minutes of the script
SCRATCH CLUB SAVE PROTOCOL: Zero retention attempts were made on any of the 5 cancellation calls processed by the setter team
DOWNSELL OFFER FOR DQ'D LEADS: For prospects who self-DQ on price or play frequency, there is currently no fallback offer
TARGETED COACHING — JULIUS AND HEINRICH: Combined 12 declines vs 3 bookings in this session
recommendations: Add a Price Pre-Emption Block to the setter script: 'Great question — and I want to make sure the number I give you is actually relevant to your specific game. Give me 3 minutes.'
Add a location qualifier in the first 2 minutes: 'Just so I can point you to the closest location — what state are you in?'
Implement a Scratch Club save script for setter reps handling inbound cancellations
Introduce a downsell path (PG1 digital / online coaching) for leads who cite price or low play frequency
Schedule individual coaching sessions for Julius Vizcayno and Heinrich Abarquez

action_items.task: Write Price Pre-Emption Block for setter script
Add state/location qualifier to setter script Phase 1 opening
Build Scratch Club save micro-script for setter cancellation calls
Design downsell flow (PG1 digital) for price/frequency DQ'd leads
Schedule 1:1 coaching sessions with Julius Vizcayno and Heinrich Abarquez

transcript_excerpts.label: WINNING PATTERN — Discovery-first pivot, full script, booked (Phoebe Collado / Jane Dunwoodie)
transcript_excerpts.text: Agent: 'Since everything is customized, same goes with the cost — cost varies from person to person. The reason I called is I want to make sure the program is a good fit for you first.' [Full discovery run, TMA booked]

transcript_excerpts.label: SCRATCH CLUB BILLING SURPRISE — No save attempted
transcript_excerpts.text: Contact: 'I didn't even know that I was a member. I'd like to cancel my membership.' Agent: 'Yeah, no problem, sir. We can cancel this.' [Cancel processed immediately. No retention attempt. Zero revenue recovered.]

insights.title: ASR Team Lee – June 24 Post-Purchase PG-One Onboarding Call Analysis
insights.product: PG-One System / SF-One Driver / RS-ONE Putter / 357 Fairway Wood / Simple Strike Sequence / One-Shot Slice Fix
insights.team_member: Marwin Ray Reyes, Emmanuella Frago, Gabriella Abem, Carlo Palma, Kim Paolo Planas
insights.summary: Full analysis of ASR Team Lee outbound post-purchase follow-up calls placed on June 24, 2026. All live contacts resulted in a booked PG-One advisor appointment — a 100% live-contact booking rate. The universal pattern across every call: zero discovery questions asked, no VIP coaching offers made, and transactional (rather than consultative) delivery. Average QA scores ranged from 6/10 (Carlo Palma) to 8.5/10 (Gabriella Abem). The team excels at scheduling efficiency but leaves significant value on the table by skipping discovery and enthusiasm-building on every call.
main_findings: 100% live-contact booking rate — every connected call resulted in a confirmed PG-One advisor appointment
Zero discovery questions asked across all calls — the 4-question framework was skipped universally
No VIP coaching offers made on any call — a missed upsell opportunity on every interaction
Gabriella Abem scored highest (8.5/10) — cleanest execution and strongest appointment detail confirmation
Carlo Palma scored lowest (6–6.5/10) across multiple calls — needs tonality, enthusiasm, and discovery coaching
Emmanuella Frago scored consistently in the 7–7.5/10 range — strong on scheduling objections but weak on discovery
Marwin Ray Reyes scored 8/10 — best rapport and humor of the team
Login/access issues identified on 2+ calls — signal that onboarding email clarity needs attention
positive_patterns: Marwin Ray Reyes — Best rapport of the team: warm humor, natural conversational cadence, precise appointment details
Gabriella Abem (Tracy Clites) — Highest-scoring call (8.5/10): efficient, professional, all scheduling details confirmed cleanly
Gabriella Abem (Curt Bousum) — Excellent real-time order verification: confirmed billing concern, committed to shipping follow-up
Universal strength: appointment detail precision — all reps consistently confirmed date, time, time zone, advisor name, and Fort Lauderdale origin
customer_objections: "I'm at work right now... we're booked up and busy... I've been up since midnight." — Charles Walker (scheduling constraint)
"Is this like a requirement for purchasing the club?" — Brett Maida (obligation concern — reframed as value-add)
"I just wanted to make sure you didn't have the 357 on my order." — Curt Bousum (checkout billing concern)
"I did purchase it, but I couldn't get my login." — Christina Haven (technical access issue)
winning_language: "Included with your [product], you also have access to our PG-One system — it's a personalized game improvement system that's going to have you working on the right things in the right order." (universal PG-One value intro)
"She's one of our best. She's an expert. So I suggest you ask a lot of things about the app." (Marwin Ray Reyes — advisor credibility language)
"Just in case your availability changes, you already have my number — just send me a text, call me back, or voicemail." (rescheduling safety net — used universally)
pain_points: Login/access barriers — at least 2 customers had not downloaded the app or had login difficulties
Busy schedules — most common scheduling objection across all calls
Checkout confusion — promotional upsell during checkout confused at least 1 customer about charges
Low app engagement — several customers had not watched training videos before the call
buying_triggers: Advisor credibility framing — naming the advisor, mentioning Fort Lauderdale origin, describing the call as personalized increased commitment
Flexibility in scheduling — offering morning/afternoon/evening and multi-day options was the key unlock for scheduling objections
Service framing — positioning the advisor call as 'help' rather than a sales call reduced resistance on every call
opportunities: DISCOVERY BEFORE SCHEDULING: Add 1–2 discovery questions before scheduling to personalize the advisor call and increase show-up rates
VIP COACHING OFFER: No rep offered VIP coaching on any call — post-purchase calls are ideal for introducing premium coaching
GOLF KNOWLEDGE & CREDIBILITY: Connect each product to a specific golf improvement outcome to build credibility
ENTHUSIASM & TONALITY: Multiple calls described as 'transactional' or 'flat' — energy in the close would increase show-up rates
ONBOARDING EMAIL REVIEW: Login issues on 2+ calls suggest the onboarding email may not be driving app activation
recommendations: Add 1–2 discovery questions before scheduling: 'Before I book you — what's the biggest challenge you're working on in your game right now?'
Add a VIP coaching touchpoint to the post-purchase script after booking the advisor appointment
Create product-specific credibility language (one sentence per product connecting it to a real golf outcome)
Add a pre-call activation prompt at close: 'Before your call Friday, try logging in and watching the first training video'
Schedule Carlo Palma for targeted tonality and discovery coaching
Audit the post-purchase onboarding email for login clarity and activation CTA

action_items.task: Add 1–2 discovery questions to ASR post-purchase onboarding script
Add VIP coaching touchpoint to post-purchase script close
Create product-specific credibility language (one line per product)
Schedule targeted coaching session with Carlo Palma (tonality + discovery)
Audit post-purchase onboarding email for login clarity and app activation CTA

transcript_excerpts.label: BEST CALL — Warm rapport, precise close (Marwin Ray Reyes / Steve Cedoz)
transcript_excerpts.text: Agent: 'Her name is [Advisor]. She's one of our best. She's an expert. So I suggest you ask a lot of things about the app, tips, suggestions, whatnot.' Contact: 'Okay, sounds great.' [Appointment booked. Score: 8/10]

transcript_excerpts.label: FLAT DELIVERY, NO DISCOVERY (Carlo Palma — lowest scoring call pattern)
transcript_excerpts.text: Agent: 'We just want to let you know you now have access to our PG-One. We just wanted to check back whether you have been able to download the app.' [Zero discovery, zero enthusiasm, zero VIP offer. Score: 6/10]

insights.title: PG1 Closer – Emmanuel Sandoval Deep-Discovery Close (Curt Bousum / SF-One Driver)
insights.campaign: PG1 All Access Upsell – Post-Purchase Closer
insights.summary: Emmanuel Sandoval completed a 37-minute outbound closer call with Curt Bousum (Iowa, age 70s), who had purchased the SF-One driver 2 days prior. Emmanuel answered a shipping inquiry first, then ran full discovery revealing a compelling profile: post-surgery swing issues, 15–20 yard distance loss across all irons, outside-in path causing a chronic slice, 5-wood as primary scoring club, and a goal of shooting his age. The call closed on PG-One All Access at $29/month with verbal authorization collected. Notably, Curt Bousum was booked by ASR Team Lee (Gabriella Abem) on June 24 — this call is the downstream result of that booking pipeline.
main_findings: 38-minute call: deep rapport and discovery drove a successful PG-One All Access close at $29/month
Curt Bousum: 70s, post-cardiac surgery, 15–20 yard distance loss across all irons, chronic outside-in slice, 5-wood is primary scoring club
Discovery revealed score goal: 'shoot my age someday' — a powerful emotional anchor used effectively
Rep answered the shipping question immediately (correctly) before pivoting to discovery — builds trust before the pitch
Minor compliance concern: card number was taken verbally on the call after secure link process was bypassed due to system error
Rep mentioned SwingScan AI and dedicated coach as value-adds — strong product knowledge demonstrated
Customer expressed willingness to stay if the program can help eliminate slice and improve distance
positive_patterns: Immediate trust-builder: answered the shipping question first before any pitch — customer relaxed and opened up
Deep discovery: let the customer tell his full story (surgery recovery, distance loss, intentional slice on doglegs, winter practice at Gulf Shores) before any pitch
Score goal anchor used: 'shoot my age' — emotionally resonant and made the investment feel worthwhile
Strong product knowledge: connected PG-One to eliminating slice and recovering distance, not just generic improvement language
Patient pacing: 38-minute call shows rep did not rush — let the conversation breathe
customer_objections: "I'm just kidding [about staying forever]. That's my biggest goal — probably unattainable." (aspirational deflection — handled by reinforcing small wins first)
"I've never taken a golf lesson in my life." (self-doubt — reframed as an opportunity rather than a barrier)
"I'm hesitant to make a big purchase at my age." (implicit cost concern — handled by positioning $29/month as baby steps)
winning_language: "We're gonna start by eliminating those slice curves — that's the start. And once we do that, I'm telling you, that will definitely follow." (slice-first, momentum framing)
"As advertised, you can definitely increase your distance 20 to 30 yards aside from eliminating the slice." (specific outcome promise tied to product)
"You make me a par golfer and I'll probably stay on your program forever." — Customer's own words showing emotional buy-in before the close
"I'm going to go ahead and start you with the PG-One App All Access for just one month right now for just $29." (trial framing — low risk entry)
pain_points: Post-surgery distance loss — 15–20 yards lost across all irons since cardiac recovery; 5-iron can't reach 150 yards
Chronic outside-in slice — 30+ years of baseball grip contributing to swing path issue
Equipment hesitation — 20–25 year old Tour's Edge set; doesn't want to invest in full set at his age
Self-taught limitations — no formal lessons ever; has tried to self-diagnose but inconsistent results
Transition anxiety — worried that a straighter-hitting driver will confuse his intentional slice on dogleg holes
buying_triggers: 'Shoot my age' dream goal — made the investment feel meaningful even at $29/month
Slice elimination promise — the #1 stated reason for buying the SF-One, so connecting PG-One to this directly was the key close
Distance recovery language — 20–30 yards more distance resonated deeply given his documented distance loss
SwingScan AI + dedicated coach — tangible, high-value features that justified the monthly charge
Trial framing — $29 for one month felt low-risk compared to the stated value
opportunities: COMPLIANCE NOTE: Card number was collected verbally mid-call after the secure link process failed. This is a process gap that should trigger a review of when verbal card collection is authorized and documented
UPSELL POTENTIAL: Customer mentioned playing at Kiva Dunes (Alabama) every winter with 25–35 friends — a potential group referral or social proof opportunity that was not explored
RETENTION HOOK: Customer said he'd stay if he can hit his 5-wood 15 yards farther and eliminate the slice — document this as his retention benchmark so the advisor call can reference it
recommendations: Flag and review the verbal card collection process used on this call — the secure link failure created a compliance gap
Pass the 'shoot my age' goal and Kiva Dunes winter group to the PG-One advisor as context for the coaching session
Document customer's specific metrics as retention benchmark: 5-wood distance improvement and slice reduction
Use this call as a training example for the closer team: discovery depth, rapport building, and aspirational goal anchoring

action_items.task: Review verbal card collection process — flag compliance gap from this call
Brief PG-One advisor with Curt Bousum's full profile before coaching session
Add this call as a positive training example in closer training library

transcript_excerpts.label: DISCOVERY DEPTH — Customer opens up after shipping question answered (Emmanuel Sandoval / Curt Bousum)
transcript_excerpts.text: Contact: 'My 7-iron used to go right at 150, 155 yards. Now I can't hit my 5-iron 150 yards. I've probably lost 15 or 20 yards on all of my irons.' Agent: 'If we can get rid of those slices, fix your distance — how many shots in a round would that save you?' Contact: 'Probably at least 5 or 6, maybe on a good day maybe 8 or 10.' [Discovery revealed. Score goal: 'shoot my age someday.']

transcript_excerpts.label: CLOSE — Trial framing lands after deep rapport (Emmanuel Sandoval)
transcript_excerpts.text: Agent: 'I'm going to go ahead and start you with the PG-One App All Access for just one month right now for just $29. Would you like to use the same card you used online?' Contact: 'Yeah, that's what I was going to ask you, if you just use that card.' [Authorization collected. $29/month close confirmed.]

insights.title: Internal Setter – Julius Vizcayno Rebounds with TMA Booking (Mark Roberts / NY)
insights.campaign: TMA Outbound – Internal Setter Team Philip
insights.offer: TMA Transformation Golf Academy – Zoom Consultation
insights.summary: Julius Vizcayno — flagged in the June 24 analysis as the team's weakest performer (7 declines / 2 bookings) — executed a strong TMA booking call on June 25 with Mark Roberts, a 65-year-old NY golfer shooting 90s. Julius ran full discovery, used root-flaw language, deployed the 'which one are you?' identity close, and booked a 5PM ET Friday Zoom with the advisor. The call was completed despite the prospect noting a 9AM appointment constraint at the 10-minute mark. This is a measurable improvement from June 24 patterns and demonstrates the impact of coaching.
main_findings: Julius Vizcayno booked a TMA Zoom consultation — a direct improvement from his June 24 performance (7 declines / 2 bookings)
Full discovery executed: slice + outside-in, chipping inconsistency (chunking), distance decline with age, 15 handicap goal
Root-flaw language deployed: 'we don't just fix the miss — we find the root cause underneath it all that keeps pulling everything else'
'Which one are you?' identity close used — prospect self-identified as closer to the 'serious golfer' category
Booking completed under time pressure: Mark Roberts noted a 9AM appointment but Julius secured the Zoom slot at 5PM Friday before ending the call
Social proof used: '9,000 golfers,' top 10/20 coaches, 30+ locations nationwide
Prospect profile: 65 years old, plays weekly, NY (4 courses within 5 minutes), takes senior discount Mondays, played in rain-out conditions
positive_patterns: Discovery-first sequencing: Julius ran full game assessment before revealing any program details or investment — a direct correction from June 24 patterns
Root-flaw framing connected to prospect's stated frustrations: 'throwing darts at it' language validated and reframed
Time pressure handled: prospect flagged 9AM appointment at ~10 min mark; Julius pivoted efficiently to booking close and Zoom invite confirmation
Warm rapport: weather chat, local course conversation, and genuine curiosity about Mark's game extended the call naturally
Zoom link sent in real-time during call and confirmed with prospect
customer_objections: "I've got an appointment at 9, by the way. I've only got maybe 5 or 10 minutes at the most here." (time constraint at 10-minute mark — Julius compressed close efficiently)
"I don't know how big a part of my life I want golf to be." (commitment uncertainty — addressed with 'which one are you?' identity close)
"Things haven't fully clicked yet." (implied frustration with prior improvement attempts — validated and reframed as the reason for the program)
winning_language: "We've worked with over 9,000 golfers. One thing that separates us from local lessons or YouTube is we don't just fix the miss — we find the root cause, the one thing underneath it all that keeps pulling everything else off." (root-flaw hook)

"There's really two kinds of golfers. There's golfers that go out there, socialize, have some beers. And there's guys who, if they're honest, know they're capable of playing better and are actually ready to do something about it. Which one are you?" (identity close)
"Just so we're on the same page — this isn't like a one-off clinic. It's a structured program with elite instruction and coaching support built in over time." (program framing vs. one-time lesson)

"Come ready and be straight with [advisor]. He's really sharp — the more honest you are about your game, the better he can put something together for you that's actually going to move the needle." (advisor credentialing + authenticity prompt)
pain_points: Slice + direction inconsistency — either slices or hits straight-left; outside-in path issue never diagnosed
Chipping inconsistency — chunks, can't consistently hit ball before ground, shows up especially on 50–60 yard shots
Age-related distance decline — 'seeing it most in the drives' and doesn't know if it's fixable
Video overload — 'I've watched too much of that. I'm just throwing darts at it' — needs structure, not more content
Practice deficit — knows he should go to the range more but doesn't; watching videos isn't translating to course improvement
buying_triggers: Root-flaw diagnosis — the idea that one root cause explains all misses resonated strongly with his 'throwing darts' frustration
Structured program framing vs. YouTube/lessons — the idea that he'd stop guessing and get a game plan was the key unlock
Local proximity — '30+ locations' with 'most are just a quick drive' reduced location objection risk
Identity close — self-identifying as a 'serious golfer' created personal ownership of the next step
opportunities: JULIUS AS A TRAINING CASE STUDY: This call is an ideal positive example to share with the team — same rep, measurable improvement in script adherence in 24 hours
FOLLOW-UP COACHING NEEDED: Julius still needs to develop the price pre-emption block and objection reframing — this call succeeded partly because no hard objections arose

PROSPECT CONTEXT FOR ADVISOR: Mark Roberts is time-constrained (9AM appointment culture), plays Monday senior discounts, has 4 courses within 5 minutes — advisor should frame TMA as efficient, not time-consuming
recommendations: Use this Julius Vizcayno call as a positive training example in team debrief — contrast with June 24 performance to show measurable improvement
Continue 1:1 coaching with Julius on price pre-emption and objection reframing — this booking relied on a cooperative prospect; harder objections will test the new patterns
Brief the Zoom advisor with Mark Roberts' full profile: age 65, NY, plays weekly, Monday senior discount, slice + chipping issues, 'throwing darts at it' frustration, 15 handicap goal

action_items.task: Share Julius June 25 call as positive training example in team debrief
Continue Julius coaching: price pre-emption and harder objection scenarios
Brief TMA advisor with Mark Roberts full discovery profile before Friday 5PM Zoom

transcript_excerpts.label: ROOT-FLAW HOOK DEPLOYED — Validates 'throwing darts' frustration (Julius Vizcayno / Mark Roberts)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'I kind of feel like I've watched too much of that. I'm just throwing darts at it.'

Agent: 'We've worked with over 9,000 golfers. One thing that separates us — we don't just fix the miss. We find that root cause, the one thing underneath it all that keeps pulling everything else off. Has anyone ever diagnosed that for you?'

Contact: 'No, 5 or 6 years ago I took a series of lessons. He's since passed away. I'm at a point where I need to decide how serious I want to be.' [Identity close opportunity opened]

transcript_excerpts.label: IDENTITY CLOSE + TIME CLOSE UNDER PRESSURE (Julius Vizcayno)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Agent: 'There's really two kinds of golfers... Which one are you right now?'

Contact: 'I'm closer to the second one, but I don't know how big a part of my life I want golf to be.'

Agent: 'The program is exactly who we built this for — golfers in your exact situation. Would you be open to speaking with my advisor? Just a 30-45 minute Zoom call.'

Contact: 'Yeah, that'd be fine.' [5PM Friday booked despite 9AM appointment constraint]

insights.title: ASR Team Lee – June 25 Post-Purchase Onboarding (Continued Pattern Analysis)
insights.summary: ASR Team Lee continued June 25 post-purchase follow-up calls. Gabriella Abem scored 8/10 on a clean outbound call with Keith Meiler (PA, SF-One Driver), booking a 4:00 PM ET evening slot. The June 24 patterns persist: zero discovery questions, no VIP coaching offer, and scheduling-only execution. However, Gabriella continues to perform as the team's top scorer with clean execution, time zone confirmation, and multiple slot options offered. The discovery gap and VIP coaching gap remain the primary improvement areas across the team.
main_findings: Gabriella Abem scored 8/10 — consistent with her June 24 performance; strongest rep on the team
Zero discovery questions asked — the 4-question framework gap continues from June 24 with no change
No VIP coaching offer — missed upsell opportunity persists across all June 25 ASR calls
Call completed efficiently in 2:34 — professional, clear scheduling execution
Keith Meiler asked a clarifying question ('What's the chat about?') — handled well with a benefit-focused response
Multiple time slots offered (4:00 PM, 6:20 PM, 6:40 PM, 7:00 PM ET) — good scheduling flexibility demonstrated
June 24 recommendation (add discovery + VIP offer to script) not yet implemented — pattern unchanged day-over-day
positive_patterns: Clean, professional execution: recording consent, purchase confirmation, PG-One explanation, time zone check, slot options, advisor credentialing — all completed
Handled the 'what's the chat about?' clarifying question with a benefit-focused, non-defensive answer
Multiple evening slots offered — customer-centric scheduling approach
Fort Lauderdale origin and advisor name confirmed — full appointment detail precision
customer_objections: "What's the chat about?" — Keith Meiler (curiosity, not resistance — handled with clear value explanation)
winning_language: "It's our step-by-step game improvement system that looks at your specific game and builds your club practice plan. It's designed to help you improve with the club you just bought." (Gabriella Abem — clear, benefit-anchored response to 'what's the chat about?')
pain_points: Discovery gap means Keith Meiler's actual golf challenges are unknown — the advisor call will start blind without any context
buying_triggers: Benefit-focused response to curiosity question reduced friction and secured commitment without pressure
opportunities: SCRIPT UPDATE URGENCY: The discovery question gap and VIP coaching gap identified on June 24 are unchanged on June 25 — script update is needed to prevent a third day of missed opportunities
PRE-ADVISOR BRIEFING: Without discovery, advisors are receiving cold handoffs — a 1–2 question pre-booking intake would dramatically improve advisor call quality
recommendations: Escalate the script update request — the June 24 recommendations (discovery questions + VIP coaching touchpoint) have not been implemented and should be treated as urgent
Add a mandatory 'what are you hoping to improve in your game?' question before confirming the appointment slot — takes 15 seconds and gives the advisor context

action_items.task: Escalate ASR script update — discovery questions + VIP offer (overdue from June 24 recommendation)
Create pre-advisor intake question — minimum 1 golf context question before booking slot confirmed

transcript_excerpts.label: CLEAN EXECUTION — Clarifying question handled well (Gabriella Abem / Keith Meiler)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'What's the chat about?'

Agent: 'Yeah, it's about the onboarding for the PG-One, which was included when you bought the club. It's our step-by-step game improvement system that looks at your specific game and builds your club practice plan. It's designed to help you improve with the club you just bought, and our advisor will be calling to set the whole thing up for you in about 15 minutes.'

Contact: 'Okay, uh, probably tomorrow sometime would be fine.' [Booked: 4:00 PM ET. Score: 8/10]

insights.title: Customer Care – Payment Portal Friction & PG-One Billing Transparency on New Orders
insights.offer: 357 Right-Hand Ladies Flex + PG-One 30-Day Trial Bundle
insights.summary: Lucky Sardia handled a 30-minute inbound call from Roy Pearman (MI), a customer buying a 357 ladies flex hybrid as a gift for his wife. The call surfaced two systemic issues: (1) the payment portal experienced multiple card entry failures, requiring repeated re-entry attempts and eventual switch to a secondary card (AmEx), extending the call by ~15 minutes; (2) the customer proactively planned to 'take the discount and cancel the PG-One trial before being charged' — a pre-meditated trial exploit that the website's unclear billing disclosure is enabling. The order was completed at $186.75 + tax. Lucky handled the payment friction with patience but the extended call length is a customer experience and efficiency problem.
main_findings: Payment portal failures extended a 10-minute order call to 30 minutes — multiple card re-entry attempts, two links sent, customer eventually switched to AmEx
Customer explicitly pre-planned to cancel PG-One trial before charge: 'Take the discount and then cancel is a better deal, right?' — agent agreed, framing this as 'the better option'
Website checkout does not clearly disclose the $29/month recurring charge after the 30-day PG-One trial — customer was surprised to learn of ongoing charges
Agent handled PG-One billing explanation well: 30-day trial, $29/month after, cancel via email or phone — clear disclosure on the call even if not on the website
Order completed successfully: 357 right-hand ladies flex at $186.75 + tax (Father's Day bundled price) with free shipping
Customer expressed intention to cancel marketing communications and PG-One trial after receiving the club
positive_patterns: Lucky Sardia demonstrated exceptional patience throughout extended payment portal troubleshooting — 30 minutes without frustration or pressure
Billing transparency: clearly explained the 30-day trial structure and $29/month post-trial charge when customer asked — no ambiguity on the call
Product availability confirmed quickly: 357 right-hand ladies flex verified as in stock
Order ultimately completed successfully despite significant technical friction
customer_objections: "A lot of times you do this stuff and you forget about it, then you automatically get billed." (auto-billing concern — agent validated and offered plan-to-cancel strategy)
"I don't want to get texts and emails from Performance Golf." (marketing opt-out — customer unchecked marketing boxes on the payment form)
"The website showed $249 with free stuff but didn't indicate there would be ongoing charges after the promotional period." (checkout transparency gap)
winning_language: "That's actually the better option. Once you receive the club, if you think she's not using the program, you can call or email us so that we can take care of it for you." (Lucky Sardia — honest, customer-first response to pre-meditated cancel plan)

"For you to qualify for a discount of $62.25 from a regular rate of $249, it will just be bundled to the PG-One program — free for 30 days, then $29/month after the trial." (clear billing disclosure on call)
pain_points: Payment portal card entry failures — expiration date field not accepting valid input; multiple re-entry attempts required
Website checkout clarity gap — PG-One recurring charge ($29/month after trial) not prominently disclosed at point of sale
Customer buying a gift (wife) who is unlikely to use the digital program — pre-meditated cancel is a predictable outcome of the bundle strategy for gift purchases
buying_triggers: Father's Day discount pricing ($186.75 vs $249) was the primary purchase driver
Free shipping confirmed — reduced friction for gift purchase
Agent's honesty about the cancel option increased trust and secured the order
opportunities: PAYMENT PORTAL BUG: The card expiration date field is failing for valid dates — this needs an immediate tech ticket. A 30-minute order call is a serious customer experience failure and likely costs additional orders that abandon before success

CHECKOUT BILLING DISCLOSURE: The website does not clearly communicate that the PG-One 'free stuff' converts to $29/month after 30 days. This is creating billing surprises and pre-meditated trial exploits — a checkout disclosure update is needed

GIFT PURCHASE FLOW: Customers buying for a spouse/family member are unlikely to use the digital program — a gift-purchase PG-One opt-out at checkout would prevent trial exploits and billing complaints downstream
recommendations: File an urgent tech ticket for the payment portal card expiration date field — this bug is directly causing order abandonment risk and extended call times

Update the checkout page to prominently display: 'PG-One trial is free for 30 days, then $29/month. Cancel anytime via email or phone.' This will reduce downstream billing surprises and retention calls
Explore a gift-purchase checkout option that allows buyers to opt out of the PG-One trial bundle — reduces intentional trial exploits and prevents billing disputes from gift recipients

action_items.task: File tech ticket for payment portal expiration date field bug
Update checkout page to disclose PG-One $29/month charge after trial
Explore gift-purchase PG-One opt-out option at checkout

transcript_excerpts.label: PRE-MEDITATED TRIAL EXPLOIT — Customer plans cancel before charge (Roy Pearman / Lucky Sardia)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'What you could suggest is probably take it, take the discount, and then cancel is a better deal, right? All right, let's do that.'

Agent: 'That's actually the better option. Once you receive the club, if you think she's not using the program, you can call or email us so that we can take care of it for you.'

[Billing disclosure gap creates intentional trial exploit. Customer saves $62.25; company loses $29/month subscriber before they start.]

transcript_excerpts.label: PAYMENT PORTAL FAILURE — Extended troubleshooting (Roy Pearman)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'It's telling me the card expiration date is a required field, but I do have that in. I've got the month of March, I've got the year 2029, the card number is correct, the CVV is correct — but it's not taking it.'

Agent: 'Oh, that's kind of weird. Would there be — can you remove all the card info first and let's try to retype again?'

[Process repeated 3 times across 2 links, ~15 additional minutes. Eventually completed with AmEx on second link.]

insights.title: Retention Team – June 25 Full-Day Analysis: Refunded, Saved & Declined Calls
insights.offer: VIP Coaching ($99/month) / PG-One Annual ($300) / PG-One Trial
insights.product: VIP Coaching / PG-One Membership / PG-One Annual
insights.team_member: Nonie Militar, Omar Abdallah Mahmoud, Homaboroma OrluOrlu
insights.summary: Full analysis of June 25 Retention Team Mimi calls across three disposition types: Refunded (2 calls), Saved (1 call), and Declined (2 calls). Key findings: billing surprise and unauthorized-charge perception are the dominant drivers of all contact. One customer (John Bautista) was charged $99/month for 5 months without awareness — a $495 dispute resolved with a full refund commitment. Another (Jeff Souza) had login issues from day one and never used the product. One customer (Mary Brooks) was saved simply by stopping future charges and explaining what she'd already paid for. Two declined calls were unworkable — one a DNC demand from repeated contact, one a product return. The team shows strong empathy but systemic issues (call frequency mismanagement, login barriers, unclear billing) are upstream of every call.
main_findings: REFUNDED: John Bautista — $495 dispute ($99/month × 5 months, Feb–Jun). Claims he never agreed to VIP coaching enrollment. Full refund committed. Customer open to re-engaging after refund. (Nonie Militar, 14:25 call, score 7.5/10)

REFUNDED: Jeff Souza — PG-One Annual cancellation ($300/year). Login issues from day one. Only reached driving range once. Plays 2–3 times/year. Declined $50 and $100 discount offers. Refunded. (Nonie Militar, 6:13 call, score 7.5/10)

SAVED: Mary Brooks — Inbound, billing alert for $322. Proactively cancelled future charges. Clarified she retains full year of training platform + GPS already paid. Customer satisfied. (Omar Abdallah Mahmoud, 2:46 call, score 7/10)
DECLINED: James Keyton — Outbound. Customer stated he had already spoken to 2 people about this and was being called 'every day.' Firm DNC demand. Score: 1/10. (Homaboroma OrluOrlu)
DECLINED: Kyle Maday — Outbound. Club didn't work, being returned. Not interested in any program. No further engagement. Professional handling. (Nonie Militar, ~1 min)
Call frequency management is a critical failure — James Keyton's call should never have been made given prior contact history in the CRM
Login barriers appear on 2 of 5 calls — Jeff Souza had login issues 'from day one'; John Bautista's wife referenced trial access she couldn't fully navigate
positive_patterns: Nonie Militar — John Bautista call: exceptional de-escalation on a complex $495 billing dispute. Empathy, account review, value re-anchor, and respect for customer's 'refund first' boundary. Customer left the call expressing willingness to re-engage.

Omar Abdallah Mahmoud — Mary Brooks call: efficient, non-defensive resolution. Stopped charges immediately, clarified what was already paid for, sent confirmation. Customer satisfied in under 3 minutes.
Nonie Militar — Kyle Maday decline: clean, professional close on an unwinnable call. No pressure, acknowledged the return, wished the customer well.
All agents maintained professional, calm tone even under frustration from customers
customer_objections: "I never verbally or through an email or app or any kind of correspondence signed up for anything." — John Bautista ($495 VIP coaching dispute)
"The approach — to find out without me actually giving my agreement or consignment and then I was charged — that's what I don't think was fair." — John Bautista
"I've never been able to really log into the account anyways. From day one I had issues." — Jeff Souza (login barrier + low usage)
"I maybe play golf 2 to 3 times a year. The membership isn't really for me." — Jeff Souza (play frequency mismatch)
"I do not want that, and I don't want to be charged that. I've already paid a bunch of money, and I'm not gonna pay anymore." — Mary Brooks ($322 billing alert)
"Sir, I've had this conversation with two people. Please, please stop calling me every day. Please." — James Keyton (call fatigue — DNC demand)
"The only reason I got it was to try the club, and the club just didn't work for me, so I'm sending it back." — Kyle Maday (product return, not program issue)
winning_language: "My goal isn't to talk you into keeping something that you don't want. I just want to make sure you have all the information about the membership, the coaching, and your options so you can make the best decision for your game." (Nonie Militar — trust-building frame that de-escalated John Bautista)

"I completely respect that decision. My responsibility was simply to make sure that you understood what was included and had the opportunity to see the value before making a final choice." (Nonie Militar — graceful close on unwinnable calls)
"I will cancel all future subscriptions and send you a confirmation on your email." (Omar Abdallah Mahmoud — Mary Brooks — clear, immediate resolution that satisfied the customer)
pain_points: VIP coaching enrollment without explicit consent — John Bautista's case represents a systematic enrollment-without-agreement risk that could recur at scale
Login access failures from day one — Jeff Souza had login issues throughout his membership; never used the product he was being charged for
Recurring charge confusion — all 5 calls involved some form of 'I didn't know I was being charged' or 'I didn't agree to this'
Play frequency mismatch — Jeff Souza plays 2–3 times/year; Mary Brooks is in her 70s and found the platform not relevant to her — both are product-market mismatch cases
Call frequency mismanagement — James Keyton had been contacted by multiple reps, yet another call was placed without CRM review
buying_triggers: John Bautista: expressed willingness to re-engage after refund — the relationship was not destroyed, only the billing dispute needed resolution
Mary Brooks: retaining her existing 1-year access was a 'save' — she was satisfied once the future charge threat was removed
Empathy before pitch — in both saved/partial-save cases, leading with 'my goal isn't to talk you into anything' reduced defensiveness and opened dialogue
opportunities: CRM CALL FREQUENCY CONTROL: James Keyton's call is a direct result of inadequate CRM flagging — any customer with 2+ recent contacts should be flagged as DNC or require manager approval before the next call

ENROLLMENT AUDIT: John Bautista's case raises the question: how many other customers were enrolled in VIP coaching without explicit consent? An enrollment audit of the December 4th email-conversion pipeline is recommended

LOGIN SUPPORT PROTOCOL: Jeff Souza and others with login issues from day one represent a failure of the onboarding flow — a proactive login troubleshooting step should be added to the first 48 hours of membership

PLAY FREQUENCY QUALIFIER: Customers who play 2–3 times per year or fewer are not good candidates for annual memberships — a play frequency question at checkout or trial enrollment could route these customers to a different product tier
PAUSE OPTION: Jeff Souza declined two discount offers but might have accepted a pause — the absence of a pause/freeze option continues to result in full cancellations that a pause would have retained
recommendations: Implement CRM call frequency rules: flag any contact with 2+ outbound touches in 7 days as requiring manager review before next call
Conduct an enrollment audit of the December 4th VIP coaching email-conversion pipeline to identify other potential 'enrolled without consent' cases
Add a proactive login troubleshooting step to the post-purchase onboarding flow (Days 1–3) — failed logins are silent churn drivers
Add a play frequency question to the trial enrollment flow: 'How often do you play or practice golf?' — flag low-frequency golfers for a different product recommendation
Introduce a 'pause for 60 days' option as a save tool between discount offers and full cancellation

action_items.task: Implement CRM call frequency rules — flag 2+ contacts in 7 days for manager review
Conduct enrollment audit of December VIP coaching email-conversion pipeline
Add login troubleshooting step to post-purchase onboarding flow (Days 1–3)
Add play frequency qualifier to trial enrollment flow
Build pause/freeze option as retention save tool between discount and full cancel

transcript_excerpts.label: BILLING DISPUTE — Customer demands 'refund first, reset, then re-engage' (Nonie Militar / John Bautista)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'I want all 5 months credited first, and then I'll call you back when I want that free trial to start. I don't want to mix the two. The 5 months that I was charged without knowing, I want that squared away first.'

Agent: 'I completely respect that decision. My responsibility was simply to make sure that you understood what was included.'

Contact: 'I appreciate you accommodating. I do believe in what you guys are doing. Once this gets credited, I would love to reconsider.' [Full $495 refund committed. Customer retained relationship despite billing dispute.]

transcript_excerpts.label: CALL FATIGUE — DNC demand from over-contacted customer (Homaboroma OrluOrlu / James Keyton)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Agent: 'I was just going over your account — I can see that your PG-One and VIP coaching trials were already cancelled, but I just wanted to quickly follow up before everything fully expires.'

Contact: 'Sir, sir, I've had this conversation with two people. Please, please stop calling me every day. Please, please. Okay.'

[Score: 1/10. No recovery attempted. CRM call frequency controls needed immediately.]

transcript_excerpts.label: DISCOUNT MISDIAGNOSIS — Price cut offered for behavioral objection (Nonie Militar / Jeff Souza)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'I maybe play golf 2 to 3 times a year. I can't see spending $300, never use it.'

Agent: 'How about I offer you $50 off — goes down to $249. Less than a dollar a day.'

Contact: 'I'm still all set. I'm just going to cancel it.'

Agent: 'How about $100 off — leaves you $199?'

Contact: 'No, I just know I'm not going to use it to the full potential.' [Pause option would have been correct; discounting a behavioral objection failed predictably.]

insights.title: FP-PG1 Team Remen – Product Design Feedback + 30-Day Masterclass Trial Close (John Caravolas / RS-ONE Putter)
insights.product: RS-ONE Putter / PG-One Masterclass / Clickstick Putter App
insights.summary: Daniel Aliyu (Team Remen) completed an 8-minute outbound post-purchase call with John Caravolas (MA), a customer in his 70s with serious arthritis who shoots low 80s and purchased the RS-ONE Putter. The call surfaced a genuine product design complaint: the putter's flange design prevents ball pickup after missed putts, posing a safety risk. The customer loved the putter's alignment, weighting, and grip design but had self-modified it to fix the pickup issue. Daniel acknowledged the feedback, offered to document it (photo request sent via email), successfully offered a 30-day masterclass trial (extended from the standard 7 days as a goodwill gesture), and closed at $299 annually. Score: 7.5/10.
main_findings: Product design complaint: RS-ONE Putter flange prevents ball pickup after missed putts — customer had to self-modify the club; flagged as a safety concern
Customer is a satisfied putter user despite the design issue — 'perfect putter except you can't pick the damn thing up'; alignment, weighting, and grip design all praised
Masterclass trial closed: 30 days (extended from standard 7 days as goodwill gesture), $299 annually after trial
Wedge pitch declined: customer has a full set (64° to 48°) he loves and refused to change mid-season
Customer has an existing local pro relationship — explicitly stated he won't switch coaching providers; rep correctly did not push VIP coaching
Discovery executed: shoots low 80s, focuses on short game (wedges + putting) due to arthritis in hands, off-tee game fixed
Customer offered to send photo of his self-modification — valuable product engineering feedback
Score: 7.5/10. Goal: 8.5/10. Primary gaps: didn't explore wedge pitch more creatively, didn't close on a specific annual plan confirmation
positive_patterns: Handled product complaint professionally: listened without defensiveness, acknowledged the design issue, took ownership of relaying feedback, requested documentation (photo)
Extended the trial from 7 to 30 days as a goodwill gesture — customer responded positively ('that's really nice')
Discovery executed naturally: game level, medical context, product preferences all surfaced without friction
Correctly read the room on VIP coaching: customer has a pro he works with, so rep didn't push — avoids a wasted pitch
Photo documentation request for the product modification is a smart product feedback loop — should be escalated to engineering
customer_objections: "I can't pick the ball up with this putter — it's too dangerous if you propel the ball. You could hit somebody." (design complaint — handled with empathy and documentation)
"I love my wedges and I gotta stick with them. There's no freaking way I'm changing." (wedge pitch declined — rep accepted without pressure)
"I'm very happy with the pro I'm working with up here. I don't need additional coaching." (VIP coaching declined — rep correctly pivoted away)
winning_language: "I'm going to do that for 30 days — normally members would have that for 7 days, but in your case, you've been a great member with us, so I'm going to do that for 30 days." (Daniel Aliyu — loyalty-based extension that landed as a genuine reward)
"I'll take note of that for sure. I appreciate the feedback." (product complaint acknowledgment — calm, professional, non-defensive)

"Try it out. Access all the videos whenever you get a chance. And yeah, of course, if you want to keep it active after the trial period, it's just going to be $299 annually." (clear trial-to-annual framing)
pain_points: RS-ONE Putter flange design prevents ball pickup after missed putts — affects senior golfers who can't or won't stoop repeatedly; safety concern if propelling the ball
Arthritis limiting hand usage — customer's entire game is built around minimizing hand/wrist involvement; this is why the RS-ONE grip design resonated so strongly
Too busy to watch app content — has the Clickstick Putter app but hasn't used it; time barrier to digital engagement
buying_triggers: Loyalty reward framing — '30 days because you've been a great member' felt like a personal gesture rather than a sales offer
Product-to-game fit: masterclass covers short game and putting, which is exactly what John focuses on
$299 annually positioned as comprehensive access — customer responded positively to the broad scope
opportunities: PRODUCT ENGINEERING FEEDBACK: The RS-ONE Putter ball-pickup issue is a real design flaw affecting a meaningful segment of senior golfers who can't stoop. The customer's self-modification is documented and the photo should be escalated to the product team immediately

CUSTOMER AS ADVOCATE: John Caravolas is highly articulate about the product, positive about the brand, and even got his son to keep the prior putter. He is a strong candidate for a testimonial or referral ask
PUTTER MODIFICATION PHOTO: The customer offered to send a photo of his fix — this could be valuable user-generated content and engineering input; ensure the follow-up email captures this
recommendations: Escalate the RS-ONE Putter ball-pickup design complaint to the product/engineering team — the customer's self-modification photo should be documented and reviewed
Flag John Caravolas as a potential brand advocate — he is vocal, positive, and already influenced his son to keep a Performance Golf putter
Add a senior-golfer accessibility screen to the RS-ONE product page noting the pickup design and who it's best suited for

action_items.task: Escalate RS-ONE Putter ball-pickup design complaint to product/engineering team
Follow up with John Caravolas via email to collect self-modification photo
Flag John Caravolas as a potential brand advocate / testimonial candidate

transcript_excerpts.label: PRODUCT DESIGN COMPLAINT — Ball pickup issue (Daniel Aliyu / John Caravolas)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'There is definitely a design flaw in that putter, and the flaw is you can't pick the ball up. You can't pick it up after you've — you miss a putt, you're 3 inches away or whatever. It's too dangerous if you go to pick it up quickly and you propel the ball. You could hit somebody.'

Agent: 'Gotcha. I totally appreciate the feedback.'

Contact: 'It's a perfect putter except you can't pick the damn thing up. But I did modify it so I can pick it up, because I was not gonna send it back because I like the putter so much.'

[Customer offered to send photo of self-modification. Product engineering feedback loop opportunity.]

transcript_excerpts.label: GOODWILL TRIAL EXTENSION CLOSE (Daniel Aliyu)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Agent: 'Normally members would have that for 7 days, but in your case, you've been a great member with us, so I'm going to do that for 30 days. Try it out. Access all the videos whenever you get a chance. And after 30 days, it's just going to be $299 annually to keep that access.'

Contact: 'Well, thank you. That's really nice. Okay.'

[Trial accepted. $299 annual positioned. Score: 7.5/10]
